courseTitle: Awareness of Mental Health and Dementia

questions.question: Which statement best reflects an awareness-level understanding of mental health?
options: Only people with a diagnosed condition have mental health
Everyone has mental health, and it can change over time
Mental health only matters for younger adults
Good physical health means mental health can be ignored

questions.question: A colleague describes someone as 'a schizophrenic'. Which is the more respectful, person-first alternative?
options: The schizophrenic in room four
A psychotic case
A person living with schizophrenia
A mental patient

questions.question: Using respectful, person-first language helps reduce the stigma attached to mental health conditions.
options: True
False

questions.question: At an awareness level, which of these might a care worker notice that could suggest a person is experiencing anxiety?
options: Restlessness, a racing heart and constant worry
A calm manner and steady breathing
Improved appetite and sound sleep
Enthusiasm about an upcoming outing

questions.question: Which change might a care worker observe and report as a possible sign of depression?
options: Increased energy and interest in hobbies
Persistent low mood, loss of interest and withdrawal
Feeling tired for a single afternoon
Excitement about a family visit

questions.question: At an awareness level, bipolar disorder is best described by which of the following?
options: A single, unchanging mood
Extreme mood swings between low and high (elevated) states
Only ever feeling low
A physical illness affecting the joints

questions.question: A person experiencing psychosis may:
options: Always be a danger to other people
Be choosing to behave that way on purpose
Perceive things that are not there or hold beliefs not based in reality
Simply need to be told firmly to stop

questions.question: A care worker notices a person seems to be hearing voices that are not there. What is the appropriate awareness-level response?
options: Diagnose them with psychosis and write it in their records
Insist the voices are not real and that they must ignore them
Ignore it, as it is not the care worker's concern
Respond calmly and reassuringly, then report the observation to a senior or nurse

questions.question: It is part of a care worker's role to formally diagnose mental health conditions.
options: True
False

questions.question: Select all that apply. Which of the following help promote positive mental wellbeing?
options: Social connection with other people
A sense of purpose and meaningful activity
Being isolated and having every choice made for them
Having a sense of control over daily life

questions.question: Complete: Dementia is caused by ___ disease and is not a normal part of ageing.
Which statement about dementia is correct at an awareness level?
options: It is a normal and expected part of getting older
It is a progressive condition caused by disease affecting the brain
It can be fully cured with the right medication
It affects only memory and nothing else

questions.question: Select all that apply. Which of the following are recognised types of dementia?
options: Alzheimer's disease
Vascular dementia
Lewy body dementia
Frontotemporal dementia

questions.question: Vascular dementia is most associated with which of the following?
options: A build-up of proteins only
Problems with the blood supply to the brain, for example after strokes
The normal ageing process
An infection of the lungs

questions.question: Which type of dementia is particularly linked with visual hallucinations and movement problems similar to Parkinson's?
options: Vascular dementia
Frontotemporal dementia
Lewy body dementia
Alzheimer's disease

questions.question: Even as dementia advances, which of the following tends to remain?
options: The person's identity, feelings and emotional memory
Nothing at all of the person remains
Only their most recent short-term memories
Their ability to recognise their own diagnosis

questions.question: A person living with dementia may forget the details of an event but still remember how it made them feel.
options: True
False

questions.question: Why is knowing a person's life history important when supporting someone living with dementia?
options: It is mainly needed for billing and paperwork
It helps carers see the person behind the diagnosis and provide personalised support
It removes the need to communicate with them directly
It is used to work out which type of dementia they have

questions.question: A person living with dementia is anxiously asking to 'go home', although they are already at home. Which response best connects with the feeling behind their words?
options: Firmly correct them: 'You ARE home'
Acknowledge how they are feeling and offer reassurance and comfort
Ignore the request until they stop asking
Tell them their family no longer wants them

questions.question: Which approach supports good communication with a person living with dementia?
options: Speaking quickly in long, complex sentences
Allowing plenty of time, using simple language and being patient
Testing their memory with rapid questions
Talking about them to others as though they are not present

questions.question: Complete: When communicating with a person living with dementia, it often helps to connect with the ___ behind their words rather than correcting the facts.
Select all that apply. Which of the following help create a supportive environment and meaningful activity for a person living with dementia?
options: Familiar objects and good, even lighting
Reducing excessive noise and clutter
Activities linked to the person's interests and past
Leaving them with nothing to do for long periods

questions.question: A person living with dementia becomes distressed, pacing and shouting. At an awareness level, this is best understood as:
options: Challenging behaviour that must be stopped
Communication of an unmet need
Deliberately difficult conduct
Something best ignored

questions.question: 'Challenging behaviour' is the preferred, respectful way to describe a person's distress reactions.
options: True
False

questions.question: Complete: A sudden change in a person's confusion or alertness may be ___, which is a medical emergency.
A person living with dementia becomes suddenly much more confused and drowsy over a few hours. What should a care worker do?
options: Assume it is simply their dementia getting worse
Wait until the next scheduled review to mention it
Recognise this sudden change as a possible medical emergency and escalate urgently
Record it and take no further action

questions.question: A sudden worsening of a person's confusion should never simply be dismissed as 'just their dementia'.
options: True
False

questions.question: The Mental Health Act 1983 is mainly concerned with which of the following?
options: Managing a person's household finances
The assessment, treatment and rights of people with a mental disorder, including detention where necessary
Diagnosing which type of dementia a person has
Setting employment contracts for care workers

questions.question: The Mental Capacity Act 2005 is designed to:
options: Allow staff to make every decision for anyone over 65
Protect and empower people who may lack capacity to make particular decisions for themselves
Provide a way to diagnose mental health conditions
Remove a person's right to make any choices

questions.question: Select all that apply. Which of the following help protect the rights of people who may lack capacity?
options: Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS), which make sure any restrictions are lawful and necessary
An advocate, who supports the person to express their views and represent their interests
Involving the person in decisions about their care as much as possible
Making all decisions for them without any consultation
